coredata.dc:description: It is estimated that approximately 700,000 fatalities occur annually due to infections attributed to various pathogens, which are capable of dissemination via multiple environmental vectors, including air, water, and soil. Consequently, there is an urgent need to enhance and refine rapid detection technologies for pathogens to prevent and control the spread of associated diseases. This review focuses on applying nanozymes in constructing biosensors, particularly their advancement in detecting pathogenic bacteria. Nanozymes, which are nanomaterials exhibiting enzyme-like activity, combine unique magnetic, optical, and electronic properties with structural diversity. This blend of characteristics makes them highly appealing for use in biocatalytic applications. Moreover, their nanoscale dimensions facilitate effective contact with pathogenic bacteria, leading to efficient detection and antibacterial effects. This article briefly summarizes the development, classification, and strategies for regulating the catalytic activity of nanozymes. It primarily focuses on recent advancements in constructing biosensors that utilize nanozymes as probes for sensitively detecting pathogenic bacteria. The discussion covers the development of various optical and electrochemical biosensors, including colorimetric, fluorescence, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical methods. These approaches provide a reliable solution for the sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria. Finally, the challenges and future development directions of nanozymes in pathogen detection are discussed.

row.entry: Proteins and RNA molecules synthesized by living cells exhibit high substrate specificity and catalytic potency, constituting a crucial class of biocatalysts.
A class of nanomaterials that catalyze enzyme substrates under mild or extreme conditions and convert them into products following enzyme kinetics such as the Mie equation.
Natural enzymes are proteins living organisms produce, usually found in animals and plants.
Nanozymes are a class of biomimetic nanomaterials synthesized chemically or through template-assisted methods. method.
Carbohydrates, amino acids, cofactors, and metals
Oxidoreductases, Transferases, Hydrolases, Laccases, Isomerases, Ligases, and Translocases
No specific substrate binding (except chiral nanozymes)
High catalytic activity, substrate specificity, biocompatibility, broad applicability, and negligible toxicity.
low cost, high catalytic activity, stability, adaptability to extreme conditions, long-term storability, recyclability, and tunable catalytic activity
High costs, limited stability, susceptibility to degradation, challenging large-scale production processes, and restricted functionality under harsh conditions
Narrow activity spectrum, limited catalytic specificity, poor substrate selectivity, obscure catalytic mechanism, potential toxicity concerns, and unestablished biocompatibility
Natural enzymes are mainly focused on biologically related fields such as biomolecular transformation.
In addition to biomolecular transformation, nanozymes are used in many fields such as bioelectric sensing, environmental treatment, disease diagnosis, and treatment.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nanozymatic biosensors: Awareness, regulation and pathogenic bacteria detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi, 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi, 830054, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is estimated that approximately 700,000 fatalities occur annually due to infections attributed to various pathogens, which are capable of dissemination via multiple environmental vectors, including air, water, and soil. Consequently, there is an urgent need to enhance and refine rapid detection technologies for pathogens to prevent and control the spread of associated diseases. This review focuses on applying nanozymes in constructing biosensors, particularly their advancement in detecting pathogenic bacteria. Nanozymes, which are nanomaterials exhibiting enzyme-like activity, combine unique magnetic, optical, and electronic properties with structural diversity. This blend of characteristics makes them highly appealing for use in biocatalytic applications. Moreover, their nanoscale dimensions facilitate effective contact with pathogenic bacteria, leading to efficient detection and antibacterial effects. This article briefly summarizes the development, classification, and strategies for regulating the catalytic activity of nanozymes. It primarily focuses on recent advancements in constructing biosensors that utilize nanozymes as probes for sensitively detecting pathogenic bacteria. The discussion covers the development of various optical and electrochemical biosensors, including colorimetric, fluorescence, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical methods. These approaches provide a reliable solution for the sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria. Finally, the challenges and future development directions of nanozymes in pathogen detection are discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic infections can be transmitted through various environmental vectors such as air, water, and soil. Specifically, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Legionella, and Bacillus anthracis are transmitted mainly by air [1,2]. Escherichia coli (E. coli), Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) are transmitted through water and food [3,4]. Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) is transmitted through soil [5]. The existence of these pathogens will have a serious impact on human health and economic development. Traditional pathogen detection methods, such as the standard plate method, are international standards, but there are problems such as large equipment requirements, vulnerability to contamination and time-consuming [6]. PCR technology can shorten the detection time and improve the sensitivity, but the cost is high and the operation is complex [7,8]. Although the ELISA method has high specificity and sensitivity, it is greatly affected by environmental factors and may appear false positive [9]. The phage detection method is highly selective but limited by various conditions [10,11]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop more efficient pathogen detection techniques to ensure accurate and rapid detection of these bacteria.

Since Clark and Lyons developed the first biosensor based on glucose oxidase in 1962, biosensor technology has made remarkable progress in the field of diagnosis [12]. These sensors can convert the interaction between biomarkers and recognition elements into measurable signals, which greatly promotes the detection of specific biomarkers. Nowadays, biosensors have attracted wide attention due to their high specificity, high sensitivity, simple operation, intuitive reading, miniaturization and real-time monitoring, and have been applied in many fields such as medical treatment, food safety, environmental monitoring and biological research [13]. At the same time, with the advancement of nanotechnology, new nanomaterials such as nanozymes have emerged in the field of biosensors. Multifunctional nanozymes are nanomaterials with natural enzyme activity that simultaneously possess multifunctional properties such as magnetic, optical and electronic properties as well as highly variable structures. These properties enable them to significantly improve the sensitivity and specific recognition of sensors, as well as effectively realize signal amplification, showing great potential for performance enhancement of biosensors [14,15]. Compared with traditional methods, nanozyme-based biosensors have the advantages of low cost, good stability and adjustable activity [16–18]. Its nanometer size facilitates effective contact with pathogens, improves detection and antibacterial efficiency, is particularly suitable for the needs of the food industry, hospitals and regulatory agencies, and shows great application prospects in the field of pathogen detection [19–21].

At present, there are some related reviews on the research of nanozymes in the field of pathogenic bacteria sensors, which have a certain guiding significance. For example, Hou et al. [22] reviewed the antibacterial field of nanozymes, mainly focusing on the antibacterial mechanism of nanozymes, how to enhance the bacterial binding force and antibacterial activity of nanozymes through the regulation of surface and composition. Yamac et al. [23] reviewed the application of nanozymes in the detection of clinically important pathogens, emphasizing its potential in the detection of bacteria, viruses, fungi and parasites. Zare et al. [24] from the perspective of the regulation of nano-enzyme materials, explored the catalytic activity of different regulators. However, this paper comprehensively discusses the development, classification and regulation of nanozymes. It focuses on the research of biosensors constructed by various types of nanozyme materials in the detection of pathogenic bacteria, and intuitively evaluates the advantages and disadvantages of different detection methods. It helps researchers to systematically understand nanozymes and select suitable detection methods to meet specific needs, aiming to provide useful reference and new ideas for researchers in the field of bioanalyte detection.

Over a thousand nanomaterials exhibiting enzymatic catalysis have been documented. Categorized by material type, nanozymes for detecting pathogenic bacteria fall into four primary groups: metal-based, metal oxide, carbon-based, and metal-organic framework (MOF) nanozymes. These typically manifest activities akin to catalase (CAT), peroxidase (POD), oxidase (OXD), and SOD [49](Fig. 2 ). Furthermore, researchers have created novel hybrid nanozymes by combining various types of nanomaterials, resulting in a diverse catalytic profile due to their multi-component structure and thus enriching the repertoire of nanozymes classes.

In 1894, H.J.H. Fenton first reported the generation of highly reactive hydroxyl radicals from H2O2 in the presence of metal ions, particularly Fe2+, a process known as the Fenton reaction [74]. Fenton identified several metals with unique oxygen transfer capabilities that could enhance the utilization of H2O2. Building on the Fenton reaction, in 1934 Fritz Haber and his student Joseph Joshua Weiss introduced the Haber-Weiss reaction, which postulates that H2O2 can generate hydroxyl (-OH) and superoxide (-O2-) radicals in the presence of strongly catalytic metal ions, notably Fe2+ [75]. These reactive species initiate the oxidation of peroxidase substrates, culminating in the production of colored oxidation products [76–78]. The above examples show that metal ions can replace natural enzymes to catalyze the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide and can be used as nanozymes materials. Compared with other nanozymes, metal nanozymes have unique electronic structures, superior corrosion resistance, and catalytic activity, and their relatively mature chemical synthesis methods also enable precise modulation of composition and morphology [79]. Broadly, metal-based nanozymes are categorized into two primary types: monometallic nanozymes and composite metal nanozymes, the latter consisting of alloys with two or more metallic elements.

Monometallic nanozymes have become an ideal model material in the field of biosensor construction due to their clear enzyme-like active sites, stable chemical structure, and high affinity binding sites with various biomolecules, as well as their remarkable surface plasmon resonance (SPR) characteristics, excellent optical properties, and photothermal conversion efficiency. The main reported monometallic nanozymes are Au [80], Ag [81], Pt [82], Pd [83], Ir [84], Ru [85], Fe [86], and Cu [87] NPs. Among these, nanogold and nanosilver are the most widely utilized single-metal nanozymes.

Gold nanozymes possess distinctive photoelectric, physical, and chemical attributes. Prominent among various nanomaterials, Au NPs, gold nanorods (Au NRs), and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) have been extensively employed in the fabrication of biological and chemical sensors [88]. Comotti et al. [89] initially documented the glucose oxidase-like activity of Au NPs. Jv et al. [90] discovered that cationic Au NPs could facilitate the H2O2-mediated oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in an acetic acid buffer solution (pH = 4), exhibiting activity akin to the natural enzyme horseradish peroxidase (HRP). The exceptional physicochemical properties of Au NPs, in conjunction with the research findings, position them as a superior material for enhancing colorimetric biosensors. This allows for rapid and on-site detection of pathogenic bacteria, demonstrating significant potential for effective applications in biosensing technology [91]. Peng et al. [92] developed a colorimetric biosensor by integrating Au NPs with phage receptor-binding proteins for the sensitive detection of various pathogenic bacteria. They initially bioengineered phages to express receptor-binding proteins that inherently bind to bacterial targets. Through a process of conjugation, the Au NPs were affixed to the phage surface, serving as signal enhancers to amplify the detection sensitivity. When pathogenic bacteria are present, phages specifically target their respective host species or strains. At the same time, the gold nanomaterial surface produces a change in plasmonic resonance properties that contribute to the visible color change of the solution. The detection time of the method was 1 h and the limit of detection (LOD) was about 100 CFU/mL. It should be noted that the preparation of polycrystalline gold nanomaterials is always uncontrollable under harsh conditions, and the nanoparticles tend to agglomerate, which is one of the reasons why gold nanomaterials require more effort in practical applications [93,94]. To ensure the efficient application of gold nanomaterials in biosensing, it is crucial to implement rigorous purification protocols and surface modifications to prevent particle agglomeration and other related issues. Meticulous structural design and thorough physicochemical characterization are imperative to ensure the stability and reproducibility of biomolecular detection.

Silver nanozymes have excellent optical properties, electrical conductivity, biocompatibility, catalytic activity and antibacterial ability. Compared with Au NPs of the same size, these particles have higher extinction coefficients and have particle size-dependent surface plasmon resonance, electrochemistry, catalytic activity, and Raman enhancement characteristics between 390 nm and 420 nm [95]. Compared with other precious metals, Ag NPs are also cost-effective and have been observed to have a lower tendency of non-specific aggregation [96].Wang et al. [97]developed an Ag NPs immobilized Schiff-base macrocycles as a novel nanozymes with POD-like activity, which can be used for the detection of pathogenic bacteria and antibacterial applications. Due to the excellent chemical stability and biocompatibility of Schiff base macrocycles, they provide an ideal carrier for the synthesis and application of Ag NPs [98]. This study utilizes the high electron density of the CN double bond in the Schiff base macrocycle to effectively adsorb Ag+ at the negatively charged imine sites through electrostatic interaction, and then promotes the formation of Ag NPs by in-situ reduction method. The experimental results show that the Ag/Schiff base composite can activate H2O2 to oxidize TMB to produce a blue oxTMB with a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm, thus confirming its POD-like activity. Although the antibacterial mechanism of Ag NPs has not been fully elucidated, existing studies have shown that the released Ag (I) may inactivate bacteria by interacting with thiol-containing proteins and enzymes in bacteria. It should be noted that integrating Ag NPs into biosensors presents significant challenges. Unmodified Ag NPs are susceptible to aggregation and oxidation, and they possess inherent toxicity, which can significantly diminish their catalytic efficacy and limit their applicability in practical settings. Relevant studies have also shown that their biotoxicity and biocompatibility can vary under different environmental conditions [99]. To effectively address these issues, it is essential to conduct appropriate surface modifications of Ag NPs and to employ more advanced techniques to mitigate or control their potential shortcomings.

Due to the different selectivity and multiple limitations of monometallic nanozymes in some specific assays. To develop composite metal nanozymes with high catalytic activity, high selectivity, and good stability, researchers have designed metal nanozymes with bimetallic or multimetallic structures, such as bimetallic Au@Pd [100], dumbbell-shaped bimetallic Au/Pt [101], bimetallic Au/Bi [102], bimetallic alloy Pd/Ru [103], Polymetallic Mn/Fe/Co/Ni/Cu [104]. These constructs leverage superior synergistic and electronic effects, manifesting unique catalytic attributes like improved res istance to deactivation, increased conductivity, and elevated catalytic activity.

Zhang et al. [50] developed a DNA-encoded bimetallic Au–Pt dumbbell-shaped nanozyme for efficient detection and eradication of E.coli O157: H7. The nanozyme utilizes the electronic synergistic effect between Au and Pt to enhance the catalytic activity. Au provides adjustable size and shape, good chemical stability and strong SPR effect, while Pt provides specific catalytic activity and photothermal effect. Especially under near-infrared light irradiation, Au–Pt nanozymes exhibit efficient photothermal conversion ability, generate local high temperatures to enhance catalytic efficiency, and effectively eradicate bacteria. The experimental results confirmed that the nanozyme sensor had high sensitivity and could detect and eradicate E. coli O157: H7 with 95 % efficiency within 5 min. Feng et al. [104] first reported a transition metal high-entropy POD nanozyme (HEzymes) with strong substrate affinity and high catalytic efficiency. HEzymes contain five transition metals with similar atomic radius, which are often used as catalytic active sites for redox nanozymes, including Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cu, for the detection and eradication of pathogenic bacteria. In this study, density functional theory (DFT) calculations show that the d-orbital coupling between different metal components is enhanced. The enhanced d-orbital coupling between metals increases the electron density near the Fermi level (EF) and adjusts the position of the d-band center relative to the EF, thereby improving the internal electron transfer efficiency of nanozymes, which is crucial for catalytic activity. In addition, the enhancement of d-orbital coupling also enhances the adsorption capacity of oxygen intermediates in the catalytic process, further improving the catalytic efficiency. Experimental data suggest that these HEzymes possess superior substrate affinity and catalytic efficiency, rivaling that of natural HRP. Moreover, HEzymes have been demonstrated in antimicrobial treatments, exhibiting a broad-spectrum efficacy against various pathogenic bacteria, including E. coli, S. typhimurium, S. aureus, and Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes). They function by catalyzing the production of ROS from H2O2. These findings indicate the promising potential of HEzymes as a new generation of nanozymes for use in biosensing, antimicrobial therapy, and other relevant fields.

Following the initial report on the POD-like activity of magnetic Fe3O4, there has been a surge in scholarly interest in the enzyme-like activities of Fe3O4 and other metal oxides [37]. Compared to precious metal nanomaterials, metal oxide nanozymes typically offer more cost-effective and straightforward synthesis pathways [105]. Furthermore, their low cytotoxicity and propensity for bioaccumulation in biological tissues have expanded their utility in biopharmaceutical applications [106]. The interaction of metal oxide nanozymes with reactive substrates, which often involves electron transfer and changes in oxidation states, also confers a range of distinctive properties, such as magnetic responsiveness, fluorescence emission, and multifaceted enzymatic activities [107]. Common metal oxide nanozymes include Fe3O4 [108], Co3O4 [26], NiO [57], MnO2 [109], ZnFe2O4 [110], and others.

Fe3O4 nanozymes have received much attention in biological applications such as magnetic resonance imaging [111,112], magnetic drug targeting [113], magnetic thermotherapy [114], and magnetic separation [115]. Fe3O4 nanozymes are commonly used as excellent carriers for magnetic immune separation and enrichment of pathogens in the field of microbial detection, ultimately improving the selectivity and sensitivity of analytical methods [116]. Fe3O4 NPs also have the advantages of low cost, high stability, and ease of separating. You et al. [108] reported a magnetic immune iron oxide nanoparticle (immune IONPs) with excellent colloidal stability and peroxidase-like activity for detecting foodborne pathogen E. coli O157:H7. In this study, the surface of iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) was functionalized by dextrin (Dex) to generate OxDex-IONPs. OxDex-IONPs showed POD-like activity but had better stability and tunability. At the same time, using the superparamagnetism of IONPs, the target bacteria and IONPs complex were separated from unbound IONPs by magnetophoresis. Under the action of an external magnetic field, the IONPs bound to the target bacteria form larger aggregates, showing a faster migration rate, while the unbound IONPs maintain a smaller migration rate. The detection system is compatible with real food samples. The LOD was 80 CFU/mL, and the analysis time was less than 50 min.

Co3O4 is a significant magnetic p-type semiconductor renowned for its exceptional electrocatalytic properties, stability, and magnetic characteristics [117]. Co3O4 and Fe3O4 are both transition metal oxides, but they are different in structure and properties. Co3O4 exhibits a spinel structure, while Fe3O4 has an inverse spinel structure, which leads to their different magnetic and catalytic properties. It has been verified that Fe3O4 shows strong magnetism due to its high magnetic saturation and permeability. Due to its high oxidation state, Co3O4 provides a more complex electronic structure and stronger catalytic activity. In addition, Co3O4 is superior to Fe3O4 in terms of thermal stability and chemical stability, making it more suitable for applications under extreme temperatures and chemical conditions. In 2012, Mu et al. [118] initially established that cubic Co3O4 NPs possess inherent peroxidase-like and catalase-like activities, which were effectively applied for the quantitative analysis of hydrogen peroxide and glucose. This groundbreaking finding has since attracted substantial interest in Co3O4 nanozymes biosensors. Subsequently, Liu et al. [119] fabricated a magnetic layered Co3O4 nanoflower that displayed remarkable multi-enzyme mimetic capabilities, including OXD-like, POD-like, CAT-like, and SOD-like activities. This nanomaterial was also successfully implemented for the sensitive colorimetric detection of acid phosphatase (ACP) and H2O2 in human serum samples. Owing to the superior multi-enzyme mimetic activity, facile synthesis, and outstanding stability of Co3O4 NPs, they have been further explored for the detection of pathogenic bacteria, presenting a viable alternative to noble metal-based peroxidase mimetics.

NiO has attracted wide attention because of its easy synthesis and porous structure. This porous structure increases its surface area and provides abundant active sites for catalytic reactions, thereby enhancing its application potential in the field of superconductors and photocatalysis [120,121]. Ray et al. [57] synthesized β-Ni (OH)2 nanoflowers (NFs) with porous structure by hydrothermal method, and prepared mesoporous NiO NFs by calcination. They exhibit superior POD-like activity and have been successfully applied to the highly sensitive detection of cysteine. Pandian et al. [122] considered that the size, shape and surface chemical properties of engineered nanomaterials can meet the actual needs through functional customization [123]. It has been found that Ni NPs with a concentration of 50–100 μg/mL have bactericidal activity [124]. Based on the above considerations, they developed a rapid and sensitive nickel nanoparticle photothermal nano-immunosensing analysis scheme for the specific capture and detection of S. typhimurium. It has the LOD was10 CFU/mL and the linear range was 10–106 CFU/mL. NiOGs exhibit photothermal effect under laser irradiation, and generate single-line oxygen within 5 min, effectively killing bacteria.

While metal-based nanozymes have such superior detection properties, they also have some drawbacks that are more difficult to control. Regardless of the decorative design of the metal-based nanomaterials, the leaching of metal ions cannot be completely stopped due to the solubilization of the metal's active site during the electron transfer activation process [125]. This inherent disadvantage is a driving force for, investigating and studying novel metal-free nanozymes.

Carbon-based materials have diverse structures and modification capabilities due to their diverse electronic hybrid orbitals (sp, sp2, sp3). Common forms include graphene [126], diamond [127], carbon nanotubes [128], fullerenes [129], and amorphous carbon [130]. These materials have attracted much attention due to their simulated extracellular matrix, many active sites and good biocompatibility. In addition to diamond, most carbon materials exhibit enzyme-like activities, such as POD-like [131], OXD-like [132], and SOD-like [133]. The electronic properties and wide absorption spectra of carbon materials make them excellent in optics, thermodynamics, acoustics and magnetism. Therefore, carbon-based nanozymes have broad application prospects in the fields of cancer diagnosis and treatment, antibacterial and biosensors [134–136].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are cylinders made of rolled-up graphene sheets, which can be categorized into single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) [137] and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) [138]. Since its discovery in 1991, CNTs have become the focus of research due to their excellent specific surface area, electrical, mechanical and electrochemical properties [138,139]. These CNTs are extremely sensitive to the interaction of biomolecules due to their excellent electrical and optical properties. In addition, their low toxicity in vivo enhances their potential in biomedical applications [140]. Currently, carbon nanotubes and their derivatives have been shown to possess enzyme-like activities, Song et al. [141] described the intrinsic POD-like activity of SWCNT in the presence of hydrogen peroxide. In addition, the effect of different oxygenation groups on the enzymatic activity of SWCNT was investigated [142]. Wang et al. [62] constructed a facile and environmentally friendly method to synthesize carbon nanotubes enriched with oxygen groups (o-CNTs) and used them as efficient peroxidase mimics for biocatalytic reactions. Their o-CNTs prepared by one-pot oxidative reflux showed excellent enzymatic activity over a wide pH range and good antimicrobial effects in both vitro and vivo models. Meanwhile, o-CNTs and their derivatives showed low toxicity in cytotoxicity tests. This study not only contributes to the construction of novel nanozymes with high enzymatic activity but also extends the biomedical applications of o-CNT-based nanomaterials.

Compared to carbon nanotubes, graphene lacks metallic impurities, exhibits superior dispersibility, and boasts a larger specific surface area. Furthermore, graphene-based nanomaterials enhance the loading capacity for electroactive species and biomolecules, achieving a more uniform and widespread distribution of electroactive sites at a lower production cost. These characteristics, combined with its outstanding electronic and chemical properties, establish graphene as a prime candidate for biosensor development [143]. Zhang et al. [144] constructed an in vivo activated pH-responsive oxidase-like graphene nanozymes (PtCo@Graphene) for the selective treatment of Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) infection. In view of the antibacterial application of most nanozymes under physiological conditions, such as wound infection, there are few studies on the treatment of specific environments such as Helicobacter pylori in the stomach. The nanozymes resisted gastric acid under strongly acidic conditions (pH = 0.9–1.5) and still had high enzyme-like activity. When H. pylori is present, it can specifically produce ROS in the gastric juice, detecting and killing H. pylori with little or no side effects on normal tissues and commensal bacteria. Its high targeting ability and bactericidal effect against H. pylori were also demonstrated in in vitro experiments. Pandey et al. [145] developed a graphene-based electrochemical biosensor for the sensitive and label-free detection of E. coli. This biosensor integrates graphene with microfluidic chip electrodes and is biofunctionalized with specific antibodies. It operates on the principle of detecting dielectric changes (capacitance/impedance) following the capture of pathogenic bacteria on graphene-modified sensor surfaces, enabling the sensitive and specific detection of E. coli O157:H7 without the need for chemical mediators. Experimental results showed the LOD was 10–100 cells/mL and the linear range was 10–107 cells/mL for E. coli O157:H7, indicating rapid and accurate in situ bacterial detection capabilities.

Although graphene is chemically stable, it is susceptible to aggregation. To mitigate this issue, the surface of graphene nanomaterials is typically functionalized, with graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) being the most prevalent functionalized forms. Related Research has indicated that rGO-modified electrodes exhibit enhanced electrochemical responses to certain bioactive molecules [146]. Du et al. [147] prepared a rGO field effect transistor (FET) electronic biosensor by an in situ thermal reduction method for rapid and sensitive detection of environmental pathogens. In this study, the rGO-FET sensor was rapidly prepared by an in situ thermal reduction method. The surface-functionalized rGO-FET biosensor exhibited high selectivity, good specificity, and stability, and could detect E. coli O157:H7 as low as 1.4 CFU/mL in 46 s. Most importantly, the rGO-FET biosensor can directly detect pathogenic microorganisms in real samples without any pre-treatment.

Typical CDs are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanomaterials, typically under 20 nm in size, composed of a sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon framework and enriched with functional groups or polymeric chains [148–150]. These surface moieties, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups, endow CDs with superior water solubility and compatibility with other materials [151]. The presence of diverse functional groups enables facile modification with organic or polymeric species, expanding their applicability in sensing [152,153]. CDs also boast a rapid synthesis process, a broad range of precursors, a high fluorescence quantum yield, low toxicity, robust resistance to photobleaching, and chemical stability [154,155]. Li et al. [156] classified carbon dots into carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nano-dots (CNDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), and carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) based on the diversity of carbon nanostructures, and they generally have enzyme-like activities. Nowadays, CDs-based nanozymes have been widely used in the fields of bioimaging, biomedicine, chemical sensing, and biotherapeutics [157,158]. Among them, CQDs and GQDs are the most widely used CDs materials for building biosensors.

Xu et al. [159] first discovered CQDs during the processing of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004. These are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm, which exhibit tunable fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light. CQDs are recognized as environmentally friendly materials with excellent electrical conductivity, chemical stability, optical stability, low toxicity, and efficient photoluminescence, rivaling traditional heavy metal-semiconductor quantum dots. [160,161]. Latief et al. [162] confirmed the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functional groups on the CQDs’ surface. These functional groups endow CQDs with high solubility and biocompatibility [163,164]. Their versatility positions them as promising candidates for the development of highly sensitive, cost-effective, and easy-to-operate fluorescent probes for pathogenic bacteria detection methods. Pebdeni et al. [165] synthesized CQDs with high quantum yield from olive leaves for detecting S. aureus based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle. By covalently binding specific oligonucleotide aptamers to the CQDs and adsorbing amine groups onto Au NPs through electrostatic interactions, they synthesized specific fluorescent probes. The probes allowed for the detection of S. aureus over the linear range was 10–108 CFU/mL, with the LOD was 10 CFU/mL. This system noted for its simplicity and effectiveness, shows promise as a practical sensor for detecting other bacteria and biomolecules.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are zero-dimensional materials harmoniously blending the characteristics of both graphene and carbon dots, exhibit structural similarity to graphene, along with quantum confinement properties reminiscent of carbon dots, and pronounced edge effects [166–168]. These characteristics endow GQDs with exceptional properties, such as a high specific surface area, high carrier mobility, chemical inertness, stability, and high photo-thermal conversion efficiency. They can be considered as minute fragments of graphene [169]. The quantum confinement effect imparts to GQDs a robust fluorescence capability. The edge effect feature of graphene quantum dots allows doping impurity atoms at the edges of GQDs to modulate their fluorescence emission wavelengths This feature paves the way for a multitude of innovative applications [170]. GQDs are recognized for their excellent biocompatibility and minimal toxicity, rendering them highly suitable for a spectrum of biomedical uses, such as in the development of fluorescent probes, cancer detection, and therapeutic interventions [126]. Yuan et al. [67] constructed a handheld paper-based (μPAD) nanozymatic biosensor based on GQDs and Au NCs for the on-site detection of pathogenic bacteria, which analyzes fluorescent nanomaterials emitted from μPADs by analyzing the color intensity of the μPAD to read the signal and quantify the analyte concentration. The study used fluorescent GQDs and Au NCs nanomaterials conjugated to antibodies in the presence of pathogens, using handheld sensors to read the red, green, and blue colors emitted by the fluorescent nanomaterials to derive analytical results, rapid and sensitive detection of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa was achieved. With a reaction time of only 10 min, it can detect multiple pathogens simultaneously, improving the multiplicity of tests.

Although carbon-based nanozymes are favored for their excellent stability, biocompatibility and easy functionalization, they still face problems such as unbreakthrough in preparation methods, large-scale production limited by reaction conditions, and insufficient research on catalytic kinetics and mechanism in practical applications. In addition, its biosafety and potential toxicity have not been fully explored, which may be an obstacle to its clinical application.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials formed by self-assembly of metal ions and organic ligands. They are known for their high structural diversity, adjustable pore size and large specific surface area. They are widely used in gas storage, separation, catalysis and drug delivery [171]. The mimetic enzyme activity of MOFs is mainly due to two factors: one is that MOFs containing metal centers such as Fe, Cu, Co, Ni and Ce exhibit enzyme-like catalytic activity, and the redox activity of these metal ions promotes the electron transfer process; second, some organic ligands in MOFs act as electron mediators, which can accept and transfer electrons to simulate natural enzyme-catalyzed reactions [172]. These properties make MOFs a powerful candidate for advanced catalysis and chemical conversion. Xing et al. [173] synthesized a platinum nanoparticle-modified metal-organic framework (Pt–PCN-224) for the microfluidic detection of food-borne bacteria. The material was prepared by the in-situ reduction of Pt NPs onto zirconium-based PCN-224 MOF and coated with polyethylene glycol to enhance stability and antibody conjugation. Pt–PCN-224 showed stable POD-like activity and was efficiently modified with antibodies for specific E. coli O157:H7 detection. The biosensor catalyzed the oxidation of TMB to a visible blue oxTMB in the presence of the bacteria, detectable by the naked eye and smartphone imaging. It demonstrated the linear range was 2.93 × 102–2.93 × 108 CFU/mL and the LOD was 2 CFU/mL within 1 h for E. coli O157:H7 in various food samples. This work highlights the potential of single-atom nanoparticle-loaded MOF composites in biosensing for rapid, on-site food safety analysis.

Prussian blue (PB), also known as ferricyanide (Fe4 3+[Fe2+(CN)6]3-·nH2O), contains Fe3+ and ferricyanide ions. PB possess a wide range of enzymatic activities (POD, CAT, and SOD-like activities), good photothermal properties, abundant redox potentials, and the ability to scavenge ROS [174]. Similar to Fe3O4 nanozymes, PB nanozymes (PBNZs) exhibit peroxidase properties in acidic environments and catalase properties in neutral and alkaline environments. In addition, PBNZs have superoxide dismutase-like effects under different pH conditions and can effectively scavenge superoxide anion radicals. Unlike other nanozymes (such as Fe3O4), the POD-like activity of PBNZs is not derived from the Fenton reaction, which produces hydroxyl radicals (·OH) by catalyzing H2O2. The results of electron spin resonance (ESR) showed that PBNZs did not catalyze the production of ·OH, but could effectively remove ·OH, indicating that the POD-like effect of PBNZs may be achieved through the charge transfer mechanism rather than the Fenton effect.

Compared with traditional nanozymatic materials, MOFs-based nanozymes have unique properties such as high enzyme activity and high sensitivity, which make them widely applicable in bacterial detection. As an emerging crystalline material, MOF materials still have some challenges and problems. Due to the complex structure of MOFs, their stability tends to be low, and they are prone to problems such as structural disintegration or ligand bond breaking, which affect their application performance. Their preparation and synthesis techniques are facing some challenges, such as how to improve the preparation efficiency and reduce the preparation cost are hot spots for future research.

Single-atom nanozymes are a class of highly efficient nanomaterials that mimic biological enzymes formed by the dispersion of isolated metal atoms on the carrier. These metal atoms act as catalytic centers and participate in catalytic reactions through electronic effects, coordination and redox activity [175]. Carbon-based materials are commonly used carriers. By adjusting the types of metal atoms and coordination ligands, the active sites of single-atom nanozymes can be accurately regulated to achieve fine control of enzyme catalytic behavior [176]. It is worth noting that single-atom nanozymes with Fe–N coordination exhibit enhanced POD-like activity, which is attributed to the unique Fe–N coordination similar to hemoglobin, and its catalytic efficiency exceeds that of traditional nanozymes [177].

The enzyme-like activity of single-atom nanozymes can be measured by enzymology. By analyzing the obtained enzyme-like activity and enzyme kinetic constants, combined with theoretical simulation methods such as substrate adsorption and density functional theory, the influencing factors of enzyme-like activity can be quantitatively calculated, and can be optimized and adjusted to obtain ideal nanozyme materials [178]. Yang et al. [179] studied the application of nitrogen vacancy-regulated tungsten nitride (WN) nanozymes in the treatment of bacterial infections. Through density functional theory calculations, they revealed the key role of nitrogen in regulating WN nanozyme activity. The introduction of nitrogen vacancies not only enhances the enzyme activity of WN, but also reduces the energy barrier of ROS generation by stabilizing the OH∗ lattice adsorption state and promoting the desorption kinetics, thereby improving the catalytic efficiency. The nanoflower-like structure of WN nanozymes helps to tightly bind to the surface of bacteria, capture bacteria through physical damage and topological effects, and quickly trigger free radical reactions to accurately inactivate bacteria, effectively inhibit biofilm formation, and inhibit bacterial proliferation.

Peptide nanozymes (PepNzymes) include single peptides with enzyme-like activity, peptide-based nanostructures, and peptide-based nanozymes. Compared with traditional enzymes, peptide-based nanozymes have higher stability under extreme conditions, simpler synthesis, higher cost-effectiveness, stronger adjustability, lower immunogenicity, and biocompatibility. These characteristics make them very promising in the fields of biotechnology, medicine and industry [180,181].

PepNzymes have a variety of enzyme catalytic activities, and its catalytic mechanism is mainly in three aspects: (i) active center simulation: PepNzymes mimic the active center of natural enzymes by designing specific peptide sequences, which can be folded or assembled into active sites similar to natural enzymes to replicate their catalytic functions. (ii) Structural self-assembly: PepNzymes utilize the inherent properties of peptides to form customizable nanostructures with specific functions. These structures can provide structural support and protection, and enhance the stability and catalytic efficiency of peptides. (iii) Integration of metal ions or nanozymes: PepNzymes enhance function by integrating metal ions or nanozymes (such as metals, metal oxides or carbon dots). These nanozymes are usually used as the main active sites, and peptides are used as scaffolds to enhance stability and functionalization [182]. Soria-Carrera et al. [183] synthesized a self-assembled hybrid material POMlymers that covalently combines polyoxometalates (POMs) with polypeptide chains. The material combines POMs with polypeptide chains by covalent bonds to form nanoparticles with POD-like activity. The self-assembly process involves electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, wrapping POMs in a hydrophobic core, providing an optimized environment for catalytic reactions. POMlymers not only enhance the catalytic activity of POMs, but also show antibacterial and anti-biofilm activity against S.epidermidis, and affect the stress response and biofilm formation of bacteria by regulating the production of reactive oxygen species. The presence of covalent bonds is essential for maintaining the stability and catalytic activity of POMlymers NPs, making them an effective antibacterial material that helps to inhibit bacterial growth and biofilm formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategies for regulating the catalytic activity of nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: As various nanozymes with distinct catalytic functions have been synthesized, their efficiency and specificity remain inferior to natural enzymes. To surpass the limitations inherent in natural enzymes and enhance nanozymes performance, researchers must employ diverse strategies to modify nanomaterials, thereby augmenting nanozymes activity. This section focuses on strategies for enhancing nanozymes performance through structural and environmental optimization (Fig. 3 ).

Numerous studies have established that particle size is a pivotal factor dictating the catalytic activity of nanozymes, a phenomenon referred to as the size effect. Generally, smaller nanozymes exhibit a higher surface area-to-volume ratio, unveiling more active sites to facilitate interactions with substrates and thus enhancing catalytic activity. [188]. Sun et al. [189] demonstrated that the catalytic activity of various carbon-based nanozymes is size-dependent. Zheng et al. [190] reported that smaller graphene dots (GDs) display more robust horseradish POD-like activity than larger GO, while also exhibiting high diffusivity and stability in biological media. It is crucial to note that reducing the size of nanozymes to a certain extent may lead to aggregation, thereby reducing the specific surface area and diminishing catalytic activity. Seong et al. [184] investigated the synthesis of Au NPs with finely tunable sizes on silica templates and their nanozymes properties. They discovered that when the size of Au NPs is too small, it results in insufficient reaction surface area, leading to relatively weak POD-like activity for SiO2@Au NPs [191,192].

Composition is the primary factor that alters the physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials. The synergistic effect of hybridized components formed through alloying strategies can effectively facilitate electron transfer and efficiently utilize the individual components of nanozymes, significantly enhancing their enzyme-like activities [193]. The two dominant factors in compositional modulation are the type of elements and the ratio of components. Wei et al. [185] discovered that as the proportion of Au grown on the surface of Pd nanosheets gradually increased, the CAT-like, POD-like, and OXD-like activities of Au/Pd nanosheets gradually decreased. In contrast, the installation of Pt nanodots on the surface of Pd nanosheets enhanced all three activities. Furthermore, Pd/Pt alloy nanoplates exhibited higher POD-like activity than Pd nanozymes and Pt nanozymes alone or their simple mixtures, indicating that the co-catalytic interaction between Pd and Pt contributes to the enhancement of catalytic activity. Pd NPs possess oxidase-like activity in the dark but face challenges in biomedical applications within the visible and near-infrared spectrum. Fan et al. [194] found that Au@Pd NRs nanozymes, fabricated by wrapping a layer of Pd around Au nanorod cores, exhibited strong oxidase-like activity in the near-infrared region.

Similar to the size effect observed in nanozymes, morphology is another key factor that significantly affects the catalytic activity of nanomaterials. The shape-specific catalytic behavior of nanozymes can be reasonably explained by the change in the number of dangling bonds and their different atomic arrangements. These properties can effectively regulate the activity and selectivity of nanozymes, thus affecting their overall catalytic performance [195]. Lou-Franco et al. [196] have comprehensively reviewed the POD-like activities of various morphologies of typical Au nanozymes. They concluded that morphologies with higher surface-to-volume ratios are likely to exhibit enhanced catalytic performance, potentially due to the increased presence of low-coordinated gold atoms within the nanomaterials. Yung et al. [186] explored the surface-dependent activity of cerium dioxide (CeO2) nanozymes by manipulating the coordination and electronic structures of Ce sites on the CeO2 surface. In this study, three CeO2 nanomaterials with distinct morphologies were synthesized: octahedra with Ce7c(111) sites, rods with Ce6c(110) sites, and cubes with Ce6c(100) sites. The results indicated that the cubic Ce6c(100) site demonstrated superior POD/CAT-like reactivity, but not hypochlorous acid oxidase-like reactivity. In contrast, the octahedral Ce7c(111) site displayed an inverse trend, showing weak POD/CAT-like reactivity but remarkably high HPO-like reactivity. This disparity is attributed to the Ce sites on CeO2 surfaces with different morphologies possessing distinct coordination/electronic structures, which influence the activation pathway of H2O2 and consequently the selectivity of the enzymatic reaction. Subsequent antimicrobial assays revealed that octahedral Ce7c(111) with HPO-like activity (generating HOBr) had superior bacterial targeting and killing efficacy compared to cubic Ce6c(100) with POD-like activity.

As the investigation of nanozymes activities has intensified, nanozymes with 0D, one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D), and three-dimensional (3D) complex architectures have been extensively studied. Liang et al. [197] have comprehensively reviewed the catalytic activities of nanozymes across different dimensionalities, including 0D (nanodots, nanorods, nanoclusters), 1D (nanorods, nanotubes), 2D (nanosheets, laminates), and 3D (metal-organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks, hierarchical structures, and hydrogels). Taking MOF-based nanomaterials as an example, while most MOF nanozymes characterized to date possess three-dimensional structures, the active sites within these 3D frameworks are often embedded within the matrix, leading to potential catalytic inefficiencies due to the lengthy diffusion pathways and substantial barriers faced by substrate molecules [198,199]. The research has revealed that 2D MOF nanozymes, characterized by their ultra-thin profiles and expansive surface areas, can significantly enhance the accessibility of substrate molecules to the active sites. 2D MOF nanozymes are anticipated to be promising candidates for the development of highly active nanozymes [200,201]. Hu et al. [202] developed an electrochemical detector utilizing functionalized 2D MOF nanozymes for the sensitive detection of S. aureus, employing a dual-recognition strategy involving vancomycin and anti-S. aureus antibodies. This detector exhibited the linear range was 10–7.5 × 107 CFU/mL and the LOD was 6 CFU/mL, outperforming many previous reports.

Surface modification of nanozymes is pivotal for modulating their catalytic activity, primarily through altering surface charge, microenvironment, and active site exposure [203–205]. Yan et al. identified ferric oxide with POD-like activity, albeit with lower catalytic efficiency compared to HRP. They hypothesized that the reduced activity of Fe3O4 nanozymes was due to the absence of an amino acid environment similar to the HRP catalytic center, despite the presence of trivalent and divalent iron ions on their surfaces, which resemble the heme group in HRP [37,133]. To enhance substrate affinity, the team investigated the covalent modification of histidine residues on Fe3O4 surfaces to create H2O2-binding sites. This modification significantly improved the POD-like activity of Fe3O4 nanozymes due to their high H2O2 affinity [206]. Subsequent studies have shown that polymer coatings, such as polydopamine, polylactic acid, and polyethyleneimine, can influence nanozymes activity. Fan et al. [187] explored the impact of surface ligand engineering on Ru nanozymes. They synthesized Ru nanozymes with various modifications, including polystyrene sulfonate, polyvinyl alcohol, polyvinyl pyridine, and polyacrylic acid, using a solvothermal approach. The PSS-modified Ru nanozymes exhibited a POD-like specific activity of 2820 U/mg, surpassing that of HRP (1305 U/mg) and other reported nanozymes by at least sixfold.

It is necessary to use external stimuli to regulate and activate certain enzyme activities from the multiple catalytic activities of nanozymes. Typically, a specific nanozyme (with a determined composition, size, shape and valence state.). The optimal enzyme activity depends on environmental factors such as p H, temperature, redox level and light [207,208]. Therefore, by applying specific stimuli, the activity of nanozymes can be precisely and selectively regulated to meet the diversity and complexity of different disease environments.

The influence of temperature on the catalytic efficacy of nanozymes is generally minimal, and these entities tend to preserve their enzymatic activity within an optimal temperature range [209]. However, the impact of pH on the catalytic activity of nanozymes is considerably more pronounced than that of temperature. Extensive research has established that nanozymes often display POD-like and OXD-like characteristics in acidic environments, while they exhibit CAT-like activity under neutral to alkaline conditions [210]. It has been observed that metal nanozymes, such as iron-based nanozymes, demonstrate POD-like activity at low pH values and CAT-like activity at high pH values [211]. At pH levels below 3, Fe(II) predominantly exists as Fe2+, which can partially convert to Fe(OH)+ or Fe(OH)2 at pH values between 3 and 4 [212]. Zhong et al. [213] have illustrated that Fe(OH)2 complexes react more efficiently with H2O2, generating a higher quantity of ·OH radicals compared to Fe2+, which corresponds to an optimal catalytic pH of 3–4 for Fenton-like nanozymes. Furthermore, other Fenton-reactive nanozymes also exhibit acid-dependent ·OH radical production, leading to acid-dependent POD-like activity [214]. While there has been substantial research on modulating pH ranges and identifying optimal reaction pH values, the majority of nanozymes studied to date are constrained to acidic conditions, which restricts their utility in experimental and biosensor applications. Consequently, future research endeavors should focus on preserving the optimal catalytic activity of peroxidase-like enzymes under near-neutral pH conditions, as well as enhancing their overall catalytic efficiency [215].
