Ions and molecules are recognized for their capacity to modulate the catalytic attributes of nanozymes, either through activation or inhibition mechanisms [216,217]. Sun et al. [218] have enhanced the POD-like activity of Au NCs by manipulating their catalytic interface using weak interactions mediated by small molecules. Drawing inspiration from the presence of imidazole structures in numerous biocatalytic centers, they conducted a screen of imidazole-containing molecules to evaluate their impact on the enzymatic activity of Au NCs. The most notable enhancement was observed with 1-methyl-2-imidazolecarboxaldehyde (MCA) on glutathione-stabilized Au NCs (GSH-Au NCs), as determined by monitoring the absorbance changes of TMB. Liu et al. [219] explored the influence of halide ions on the enzymatic activity and switching efficiency of Au NPs. The distinct affinities of halide ions for Au NPs were crucial in modulating the efficiency of halide ion-mediated switching. Iodide (I−) and bromide (Br−) were effectively inhibited at minimal ionic concentrations due to their strong interaction with gold (Au-X), whereas chloride (Cl−) demonstrated weak inhibitory effects on the enzymatic activity, and fluoride (F−) did not inhibit the Au nanozymes even at elevated ionic concentrations.

To achieve precise spatial and temporal control of enzyme reactions, significant progress has been made in the development of nano-enzymes that respond to external stimuli such as light and magnetic fields [220]. Light is a pivotal component in numerous natural biochemical reactions [221], with certain enzymes, such as DNA photolyase [222], fatty acid photodecarboxylase [223], and protochlorophyll oxidoreductase [224], being inherently light-activated [225]. Drawing inspiration from these natural light-activated enzymes, researchers have investigated the impact of light on nanozymes and identified that light-responsive nanozymes can be classified into three categories based on their selective activation at specific wavelengths: (i) UV light-induced photoisomerization; (ii) near-infrared irradiation; (iii) visible-light-driven activation. Prins et al. [226] have functionalized Au NPs with photosensitizing cofactors, enabling the selective initiation of their catalytic activity through a UV light-mediated trans-cis isomerization process. Additionally, Huang et al. [227] have described bimetallic quasi-metal-organic framework (Q-MOF Ce0.5) nanosheets that possess both oxygen vacancy-coupled redox cycling and a photosensitive energy band structure. Upon visible light irradiation, these nanosheets exhibit significantly enhanced POD-like activity.

Many nanomaterials inherently possess magnetic properties, such as Fe3O4, which can be harnessed for the precise regulation of nanozymes activity in response to a magnetic field [228]. The magnetic field as an external stimulus offers distinct advantages for clinical applications due to its exceptional biosafety, adjustable nature, and lack of tissue penetration depth limitations. Capitalizing on these properties, Fan et al. [229] have examined the influence of magnetic stimulation on the activity of iron oxide peroxidase nanozymes by fabricating a series of nanozymes with varying magneto-thermal conversion efficiencies. Their findings demonstrate that an alternating magnetic field can effectively modulate the catalytic activity of nanozymes solutions, with the enhancement of enzymatic activity being positively correlated with the materials' inherent magneto-thermal conversion efficiency.

It has been widely reported that the activity of nanozymes is highly dependent on external environmental factors. In general, the fluctuation of temperature and pH will affect the structural stability of nanozymes and their ability to bind to substrates. The change of ionic strength can adjust the catalytic efficiency of nanozymes by charge shielding or competitive binding with active sites. External energy such as light, electricity, and magnetic field can activate or inhibit the activity of nanozymes and change their electronic states. In practice, these factors often interact in complex systems, and they may produce synergistic or antagonistic effects. Appropriate temperature and pH can optimize the structure and substrate binding of nanozymes, and specific metal ions as cofactors can enhance their activity. However, extreme temperature or pH values and strong external energy may destroy the structure of nanozymes, thereby reducing their catalytic performance. The combined effects of these factors determine the efficiency and stability of nanozymes in biomedical and industrial applications [230–232].

At present, significant progress has been made in the research of nanozyme regulation strategies, and a variety of high-performance nanozymes have been successfully developed and widely used in many fields. However, the regulation and design of nanozymes mostly depend on empirical “trial and error experiments” [233]. In order to reduce the trial and error frequency in the research and development process, reduce cost consumption and improve research and development efficiency, future research needs to focus on the optimal design and controllable preparation technology of nanozymes [234]. This includes the use of advanced technologies such as machine learning and big data analysis to guide the rational design of nanozymes in order to achieve standardized production and large-scale application of nanozymes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nanozyme-based biosensors for pathogenic bacteria detection
ce:section.ce:para: Nanozyme biosensors are analytical devices constructed using nanozymes with biological recognition and catalytic ability [235]. These nanozymes have adjustable enzyme activity and a variety of species and can be selected according to different detection requirements to construct a variety of nanozymes biosensors ( Table 2).

Colorimetric sensors are favored in biosensors because of their advantages such as simple operation, low cost, intuitive visibility, no need for expensive equipment, and easy interpretation of results. The nanozymes colorimetric sensor performs qualitative and quantitative analysis of the analyte by changing the color of the substrate. These nanozymes produced corresponding color changes by reacting with specific chromogenic substrates such as TMB [236], 2,2′-azodiamine-(3-ethylbenzothiazole-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) [237], and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) [238]. This technique exhibits good operational stability and has been widely used in the qualitative and quantitative detection of pathogenic bacteria [239].

Dou et al. [240] constructed a lateral flow immunoassay (LD-LFIA) based on the "Three-To-One" multifunctional nano-enzymatic composite Fe3O4@PDA@Pt for the detection of pathogenic bacteria without labelling and with double readout. This material uses the magnetic core of Fe3O4 to quickly isolate bacteria, and the adhesion of the polydopamine (PDA) layer directly captures bacteria, omitting the need for paired antibodies in traditional detection. The POD-like activity provided by Pt NPs enhances the detection signal by catalyzing the color reaction between TMB substrate and H2O2. During the detection process, Fe3O4@PDA@Pt was mixed with E. coli O157:H7 to form a complex, which was separated by magnetic field and added dropwise to the lateral flow test strip. The antibody on the test strip was captured, and a black signal that could be observed by the naked eye was generated on the test line. At the same time, the catalytic activity of Pt NPs produced additional signals in the substrate solution, which improved the sensitivity and accuracy of the detection. In practical applications, the researchers added E. coli O157:H7 to drinking water and chicken samples. Through pretreatment and LD-LFIA detection, it was found that the color intensity of the test line increased with the increase of bacterial concentration, and the LOD was 102 CFU/mL and 103 CFU/mL, respectively. The recovery experiment showed that the method had high application potential and good reproducibility in the detection of water and chicken samples, which proved its feasibility and accuracy in the detection of actual food samples. Most of the current nanozymatic colorimetric sensors are mainly focused on the development of specific and sensitive detection of a certain pathogen, but in many environments, multiple pathogens can be present at the same time. Therefore, the development of simultaneous detection of multiple pathogenic bacteria is of great scientific interest. Inspired by the “electronic nose/tongue” strategy, Lu et al. [241] designed a colorimetric sensor array based on metal nanoparticles supported nanozymes (MNNs) for the analysis of multiple targets (Fig. 4 A). Nanozymes with different non-specific interactions were prepared by in-situ reduction of metal nanoparticles such as Au, Ag, and Pd on polypyrrole-coated molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), which effectively expanded the number of receptors. These nanozymes mimic POD-like activity and can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to produce blue oxTMB. Through statistical methods such as principal component analysis (PC), the unique colorimetric response patterns produced by different proteins and bacteria were identified by pattern recognition, which can accurately identify a variety of proteins and bacteria. The method was successfully applied to differentiate human serum samples, simulated body fluid environments, and clinical cancer samples, as well as to identify unknown protein samples and detect protein conformational changes. Experiments show that the MNNs sensor array can well distinguish 11 proteins with unique fingerprint-like patterns, and has sufficiently high sensitivity for individual proteins, with a detection limit as low as the nanomolar level. In addition, the five oral bacteria were also well separated from each other at the 107 CFU/mL level without cross-classification.

Although colorimetric biosensors are widely used due to their simplicity and high cost-effectiveness, limited sensitivity and specificity are still a major problem. The detection mechanism based on color change is affected by various factors, such as the complexity of the sample matrix and environmental conditions, which may lead to false positives or false negatives. In addition, changes in the aggregation or distribution of nanoparticles within the sensor can also cause signal fluctuations, affecting the accuracy and repeatability of the detection.

Fluorescent biosensors have obvious advantages such as simple preparation, fast response, high sensitivity, and selectivity for a wide range of targets [242]. Currently, there are three main methods for fluorescence detection of pathogenic bacteria based on nanomaterials [243]: (i) identification of bacteria by fluorescence imaging. (ii) enhancement or alteration of fluorescence properties upon binding to the target. (iii) bursting of fluorescence signals from energy receptors.

Fu et al. [244] constructed a fluorescence quenching system based on a sandwich structure of magnetic beads/Au NPs/DNA-Ag NCs nanoclusters for rapid and sensitive detection of S. typhimurium. The working principle of the sensor is to use the aptamer to specifically bind to the target bacteria, resulting in the release of Au NPs from the fluorescence quenching system, thereby inhibiting the fluorescence quenching of DNA-Ag NCs, so that the fluorescence intensity change is proportional to the bacterial concentration, achieving high selectivity and sensitivity detection of S. typhimurium. In this study, it was used to detect S. typhimurium in artificially contaminated milk. The whole detection process could be completed within 2 h and 10 min. The linear range was 7.6 × 103–7.6 × 106 CFU/mL and the LOD was 3.4 × 102 CFU/mL. It can be used as an effective tool for the detection of S. typhimurium in complex foods. Importantly, the detection system can detect different bacteria by changing the corresponding aptamers. Importantly, the detection system can detect different bacteria by changing the corresponding aptamer. Compared with metallic nanozymes, carbon-based nanozymes materials are inexpensive, low-toxicity, and have good biocompatibility, making them a promising nanozymatic biosensor material for a wide range of applications. Yang et al. [245] constructed a fluorescent immunoassay method based on carbon dot encapsulated fragile silicone nanocapsules (CDs@BONs) for ultrasensitive detection of S. aureus at the single cell level (Fig. 4B). They achieved specific recognition of S. aureus by modifying anti-S. aureus antibodies on the surface of CDs@BONs. The CDs in BONs were released by NaBH4 reduction to produce a strong fluorescence signal. Since hundreds of CDs are encapsulated in each nanocapsule, the fluorescence signal is amplified by two orders of magnitude, and the sensitivity is 108 times that of the traditional immunoassay method. In the actual detection of milk and orange juice, the recovery rate of this method is between 95 % and 105 %, the linear range of detection was 1 × 102–1 × 104 CFU/mL, and the LOD was 30 CFU/mL. These results verify the effectiveness and application potential of this method in practical food detection.

The fluorescence determination method based on nanomaterials is used for pathogen detection, which breaks the limitations of traditional methods, greatly shortens the detection time, and greatly improves the detection sensitivity. However, the problem of signal interference is the main challenge of fluorescent biosensors. When the sample matrix is complex, the interference of autofluorescence may lead to false positive results, and the photobleaching of fluorescent molecules will affect the stability of the signal. These sensors may require specialized fluorescence detection equipment, which limits their portability and application in resource-limited environments. The stability and long-term preservation of fluorescent sensors are also a challenge, especially when environmental conditions change.

Surface Enhanced Raman Sensors (SERS) has the advantages of high sensitivity, low fluorescence interference, spectral fingerprint, and non-destructive data acquisition in the aqueous environment. SERS has recently attracted much attention in the rapid detection and sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria [246]. Some nanomaterials can be used as both nanozymes and SERS probes because they have both SERS properties and mimic enzyme activity. SERS utilizes the surface plasmon resonance effect of metal nanostructures to generate strong electromagnetic field enhancement in the hot spot area, which significantly amplifies the Raman signal of adjacent molecules. As the core of biosensors, nanozymes mimic natural enzymes to catalyze specific chemical reactions. The substrate conversion products in the catalytic process can be monitored in real time by SERS and enhance the Raman signal to achieve high sensitivity and high selectivity detection of biomolecules and chemicals [247].

He et al. [248] developed SERS biosensor based on MOF and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) for the detection of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) (Fig. 5 ). Firstly, by encapsulating the ALP molecule in the MOF structure, the ALP enzyme activity was temporarily inhibited, similar to the “locked” state. Then, AuNSs were combined with OPD to form AuNSs@OPD as SERS enhanced substrate, which significantly improved the Raman signal of OPD. When ATP interacts with MOF/ALP, it will trigger the dissociation of MOF structure, release ALP, and then catalyze the conversion of OPD into oxOPD. With the increase of ATP concentration, the Raman signal (1262 cm−1) of OPD decreased accordingly, and the quantitative detection of ATP was realized. After adding different concentrations of ATP to healthy human serum and urine samples, the biosensor showed good recovery (98.56 %–105.3 %) and low relative standard deviation (1.9 %–4.4 %), which proved its effectiveness and reliability in practical biomedical detection. Zhang et al. [249] constructed a microfluidic immunoassay technique based on gold@Prussian blue nanozyme (Au@PBNP) for sensitive detection of allergen protein α-lactalbumin (α-LA) in food. This nanozyme not only exhibits POD-like activity and can catalyze ABTS to produce green ABTSox in the presence of H2O2, but also has an obvious single Raman vibration peak (2156 cm−1) in the Raman silent region (1800–2800 cm−1) of PB on its surface, making it an ideal reporter molecule for SERS analysis. In the detection process, the target α-LA was first captured by magnetic beads (MBs) containing capture antibody (Ab1), and then Au@PBNP nanozyme with detection antibody (Ab2) was introduced to form MB-α-LA-Au@PBNP sandwich complex. After removing the unbound nanozymes and antibodies by magnetic field separation and cleaning steps, the mixture of ABTS and H2O2 was injected into the microfluidic chip. The Au@PBNP nanozyme catalyzes the oxidation of ABTS to the green product ABTSox, and its color change can be visualized by the smartphone. The SERS signal of the Au@PBNP nanozyme is measured by a portable Raman spectrometer, thereby achieving a high-sensitivity dual readout detection of α-LA.

After a lot of literature research, we found that nanomaterials that can be used as SERS substrates and have enzyme activity are relatively scarce, especially in the field of pathogen detection. SERS technology has shown great application potential and guiding value in the detection of pathogenic bacteria due to its excellent sensitivity. In the future, a lot of research work needs to be carried out in this field to develop more nanomaterials with dual functions to meet the needs of pathogen detection.

Electrochemical biosensors have shown significant advantages in the field of pathogenic bacteria detection due to their characteristics of no need for complex equipment, easy miniaturization, low cost and high-efficiency analysis. They can achieve low detection limits with limited sample size, maintain good stability, and even work stably at room temperature for a long time [250]. In particular, the combination of nanozymes technology and electrochemical sensing, using the electrocatalytic properties of nanozymes, provides new impetus for the development of electrochemical biosensors [251].

Hu et al. [252] constructed an electrochemical detector based on two-dimensional metal-organic framework (2D MOF) nanozymes for ultrasensitive detection of S. aureus. The detector uses a dual recognition strategy of vancomycin and anti-S. aureus antibody (Ab2) to specifically anchor S. aureus. The 2D MOF nanozyme exhibits POD-like activity and can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of OPD to oxOPD. The peak current is generated during the electrochemical reduction of oxOPD back to OPD, and the peak current corresponds to the concentration of S. aureus. The linear range was 10–7.5 × 107 CFU/mL and the LOD was 6 CFU/mL, which can accurately distinguish S. aureus and other bacteria. Although the study did not involve the actual sample test, it provided a scientific basis for the future application of this electrochemical detector in clinical diagnosis. Future studies need to verify its performance in real biological samples, including sample preprocessing, detector stability and anti-interference ability. Li et al. [253] constructed a portable saliva sensor for the detection of Streptococcus mutans (S. mutans), a pathogen that causes dental caries (Fig. 6 ). The sensor combines S. mutans-specific Apt and S. mutans imprinted polymers (SIPs) to improve the selectivity of detection. The sensor was constructed by immobilizing aminated DNA aptamer on multi-walled carbon nanotubes (c-MWCNTs) and electrodepositing polypyrrole (PPy) and methyl blue (MB) on it. During the electrodeposition process, S. mutans was embedded in the PPy matrix to form a specific binding cavity. After treatment with lysozyme and Triton X-100, the S. mutans template was removed, leaving a cavity. MB, as a redox probe, is distributed in the PPy matrix. When S. mutans binds to the cavity, it hinders the electron transfer between MB and the electrode, resulting in a decrease in the MB reduction current signal. This change is related to the concentration of S. mutans, and quantitative analysis is achieved. The sensor has excellent selectivity and can accurately detect S. mutans in a variety of oral bacteria. It was successfully used to detect S. mutans in actual saliva samples with the LOD was 2.6 CFU/mL and a detection range was 102–109 CFU/mL.

Electrochemical sensors have the advantages of easy miniaturization, simple sample handling, and high sensitivity, but further efforts are needed to industrialize their application. The issue of specificity is a major challenge for electrochemical biosensors, which, due to the complexity of biological samples, may be interfered with by other sample components, leading to false-positive or false-negative results. Despite the advantages of cost-effectiveness and portability of electrochemical biosensors, they still require specialized equipment and trained operators, which limits their application in resource-limited environments.

Many nanomaterial sensing systems mainly use a single signal output mode. This model is easily affected by environmental and operational factors, which makes it difficult to verify the test results with each other. The difference in instrument accuracy may lead to problems such as insufficient sensitivity of the detection method. Therefore, the development of dual-mode or even multi-mode detection methods is of great significance for pathogen analysis. The dual-mode detection mechanism usually refers to a detection system that can provide two different signal outputs at the same time. The development of dual-mode detection mechanism utilizes the multi-enzyme activity of nanozymes, the synergy with biological enzymes, the binding with aptamers, and bifunctional nanomaterials. Compared with single-mode detection, they can avoid false positives and improve reliability [254,255]. You et al. [256] constructed a multifunctional platinum nanoparticle-modified phage nanozyme bioluminescent/pneumatic dual-mode biosensor platform for rapid detection of S. typhimurium activity (Fig. 7 A). The platform uses the specific recognition of phage to S. typhimurium to enhance the signal by forming a sandwich structure. PtNPs catalyze H2O2 to generate oxygen pressure signals and generate hydroxyl radicals around the bacteria, enhancing bacterial lysis and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) release, and improving bioluminescence signals. By comparing the signal difference between the total number of bacteria and the number of live bacteria, the number of dead bacteria can be calculated and the activity of S. typhimurium can be evaluated. The recoveries of S. typhimurium in pork, fish and milk samples ranged from 91.0 % to 108 %, showing the effectiveness and accuracy of the system in actual food detection. The detection of S. typhimurium in fish samples may indicate sample contamination.

Compared with single-mode and dual-mode detection, multi-mode detection can better adapt to diverse detection environments, especially in the case of limited resources. It provides more comprehensive analysis by integrating multiple signal outputs, thereby improving the comprehensiveness and reliability of detection. By carefully regulating the composition and structure of nanozymes, such as metal doping, alloying or surface modification, we can optimize their electronic structure and accessibility of active sites, thereby significantly improving their catalytic activity and enabling them to respond to multiple signal modes [257,258]. Huang et al. [259] developed a novel lateral flow immunoassay test strip (LFIA) to synthesize sea urchin-like Au–Ag@Pt NPs (UAA@P NPs) via a substitution reaction, which achieved high sensitivity for the detection and specific identification of multiple pathogenic bacteria in the blood (Fig. 7 B). These nanoparticles have unique colorimetric-SERS-photothermal-catalytic properties. Among them, the rough Pt layer gives them excellent SERS activity and catalytic performance. UAA@P NPs can absorb near-infrared (NIR) light. When they are irradiated by NIR laser, they can convert the absorbed light energy into heat energy, resulting in a local heating effect. The surface of UAA@P NPs was modified with 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid to form UAA@P/M NPs. These functionalized nanoparticles can effectively capture bacteria by covalently binding to peptidoglycan, lipopolysaccharide, and glycoprotein-containing cis-diol structures on the surface of bacteria. UAA@P/M NPs were combined with LFIA. When the sample contained the target bacteria, UAA@P/M NPs formed a complex with the bacteria, which migrated along the test strip and was captured by the capture antibody on the test line (T-line) to form a visible black band for colorimetric analysis. The LFIA was successfully applied to the detection of bacteria in blood samples and effectively identified three bacteria (E. coli, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa). The recovery rate was between 90.3 % and 108.8 %, which could distinguish infected patients and healthy individuals, showing the potential in early warning and diagnosis of bacterial infection. However, in multi-mode detection, the design of nanozymes needs to take into account their activity and stability in different detection modes. For example, some nanozymes may perform well in photoelectrochemical detection, but not in colorimetric or fluorescent detection. Therefore, understanding and optimizing the structure of nanozymes to meet specific detection needs is the key to developing effective multi-mode detection mechanisms [260].

Nanozymes have shown great potential in the detection of pathogenic bacteria due to their high sensitivity, specificity, rapid response and cost-effectiveness. In addition, the good biocompatibility of the nanozymes sensor provides the possibility for in vivo applications, further broadening its application prospects in clinical diagnosis and treatment monitoring. Nevertheless, nanozyme technology still faces some limitations in practical applications.
(i)
Nanozymes exhibit a variety of enzyme-like activities, but the relationship, synergy, or antagonism between these activities and the mechanism behind them have not been fully elucidated. To comprehensively improve the performance of nanozyme biosensors, revealing their catalytic mechanism is a crucial step.
(ii)
Given the complexity of the microenvironment in vivo, the in vivo catalytic behavior of nanozymes is difficult to be accurately regulated, and the potential biocatalytic reactions and biological interface interactions are difficult to predict. These uncertainties may affect their biosafety. Therefore, it is necessary to conduct in-depth research on the biosafety of nanozymes.
(iii)
The industrialization and intelligentization of nanozyme sensors are the key directions of the development of modern biosensor technology. By integrating nanozymes into microfluidic platforms, the goal of industrialization can be accelerated. At the same time, the combination of nanotechnology with the internet of things, artificial intelligence, and fully automated technologies enables intelligent data analysis and real-time monitoring. We can identify potential health threats earlier and take preventive measures promptly, thus playing a key role in safeguarding public health security.
